What helps most is assuming the other person can handle it. The moment I start gauging whether someone can "take" the truth, I slip into careful phrasing that helps nobody. Second is timing: uncomfortable part first, not as a soft landing at the end.
Under pressure, dishonesty rarely comes from lying. It creeps in through omissions, through picking the least confrontational number, through not asking the question when I already suspect the answer. So for me it's about noticing what I'm about to skip, and putting it on the table anyway, even if it makes the room harder.
What brings you to this question, Olive? You're right, and that pattern runs deeper than just this game. The hidden version of a problem costs more than the visible one, because it compounds. Someone has to carry it, work around it, or pretend it isn't there. That creates friction no one budgets for.
Most organizational dysfunction I've seen doesn't come from bad decisions - it comes from decisions made on incomplete pictures, because someone decided what could stay quiet. So I've learned to distrust comfort in those moments.
Where are you taking this, Olive?
